reasoning: Speaker at C (elevated), object at D (mid-level), listener at A (elevated). Object is away from speaker but not at listener, so 'itu' (away from speaker) is appropriate.
In Buru, object at D is south of the river and downhill from speaker at C. Pattern shows 'dae' is used for downhill/downstream positions.
Blue shirt at B, speaker at D, listener at G. Looking at the pattern for 'blue boat' at B which uses 'hipcom', and considering B's elevated position.
Speaker at D, candlestick at G (elevated, north of river), listener at L (far south). Object is distant from speaker, suggesting 'itu'.
G is north of river and elevated. Buru pattern shows 'aki' for uphill/northern positions.
Candlestick at elevated position G. Seri pattern for objects at elevated positions uses 'himcom'.
Black shoe at I (on ground, downhill). Seri uses 'taquih' for objects at ground level in lower positions.
Spider at F (downhill, near river). Buru uses 'dae' for downhill/downstream positions.
Speaker at D, listener at E (nearby), pot at F (downhill). Object is away from speaker toward downhill, suggesting 'itu'.
Pot at F (ground level, downhill). Seri uses 'ticop' for objects at ground/lower level.
Man sitting at K (south of river, away from both speaker and listener). Buru uses 'saka' for distant southern positions.
Tree at K where speaker is also located. Waray uses 'adi' when object is at speaker's location.
Tree at speaker's location K (ground level, south of river). Seri uses 'ticom' for standing objects at lower/speaker's level.
Lamppost at G where both speaker and listener are located. Waray uses 'adi' when object is at speaker's (and here also listener's) location.
Object at same location as both speaker and listener at G. Buru pattern shows 'naa' is used when object is at shared location or nearby both parties.

Both Waray and Buru encode distance from the speaker. Waray uses different forms based on proximity (ini=very close, adi=at speaker, itu=away, adtu=far). Buru also encodes distance but relative to geographical features.

Buru is spoken in Indonesia where river travel is important. 'Pao' appears in contexts where objects are across the river or require traveling along rivers to reach, suggesting it has taken on the meaning of 'far away'.

answer: hipquij: male person standing or in upright position; ticom: person at ground level or sitting/lying down at lower elevation; himcop: person at elevated position or standing at higher ground
reasoning: Analyzing Seri demonstratives for people: 'hipquij' is used for males (boy sitting uses himquij which is similar); 'ticom' for people at lower/ground level positions; 'himcop' for people at elevated positions. The system encodes both elevation and gender/posture.
